speaker: interviewer
text: You feel-- you're comfortable sitting there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: No problem. And when I ask why you were on a regular train, I think probably because of your--

speaker: interviewee
text: British--

speaker: interviewer
text: British.

speaker: interviewee
text: British, certainly.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll get to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the way, I'm telling you this off the-- it's not on now? We stopped in Salzburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: It is on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's on?

speaker: interviewer
text: The audio is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah, I think I'll have a drop. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three of the interview with Mr. Hyman Skulsky, tape number three. Mr. Skulsky, so you arrive at this other camp. Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a regular camp with barracks-- it's like barracks-- wooden barracks, rows of-- and very primitive, also, bunks. The grounds were-- there were plenty of grounds, but it was freezing cold outside. I have never experienced a cold like I had there. I think I had-- we had three blankets each. And on-- I put a coat, which I had, a winter coat, on top of it. I think I had five things on me to keep warm at night. It was freezing cold. Anyway, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the camp, we had a secret radio, as I mentioned before. Every night, at 7 o'clock, after dinner, we all gathered in one room and a British prisoner-- I've forgotten his name now-- used to come in to give the news. First, he gave the regular news, the German news. And then, he said-- he said, now I'll give you the rumors. That's what he called, the rumors, it is said. And this was the BBC. I never knew till close to the end-- oh, yes, on this trip, I will tell-- I'll mention it-- till we left Kreutzberg to go to Spittal, had no idea where the-- who had the radio. But somebody had it. And we got all the BBC news every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when we left Kreutzberg, I saw one man was holding a medicine ball. You know what a medicine ball is? It's about this size, quite heavy. And he was taking it along. And I thought to myself, isn't this stupid? You're running away from a camp. You're a refugee. They're taking-- with the German guards, who needs a ball like this? Anyway, he took it. He took it. When we came, when we arrived in Spittal, somebody who did know said, you know what he had in this ball? The radio set was in there. So he carried it right open under his arm. And this was the secret radio.

speaker: interviewee
text: The radio itself was about this big. I saw it afterwards. In fact, I have a picture in one of the albums of it. And that's how we got the news every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people you think were in the camp at Spittal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spittal-- 600, total. Around 660, I believe, or 650.

speaker: interviewer
text: All men?

speaker: interviewee
text: All men. In our camp, all men, yes. It was-- the same camp was in Southern-- in France for women, for British woman. In Vittel-- there was a place in France called Vittel-- and there they had a camp for women, British subjects.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was the food at that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that camp, we got-- very rarely, we got the British packages from the Red Cross, about once in a month, maybe sometimes once in three weeks. Because of the war, they couldn't-- they didn't come through. So the food was not very good. We used to get-- mornings, we got hot water, one slice of bread-- black bread-- which was-- we had to eat it, but it was terrible, the worst you've ever eaten in bread. And then, lunch, we got-- we used to call it bird food. It's made of millet or something like this, just cooked in hot water. And that was-- This was the lunch. Sometimes, they used to give us German cold herrings, bits of herrings, which was not bad. But generally, very little food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work did the people do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not have to work at all. So whoever wanted to keep busy, people had hobbies. Many people played cards all day, which I did not like. I never-- never in my life I was keen on card play. So I did other-- I used to read a lot. We had a library, by the way, from the Red Cross. We had study books. We had lectures. We had several professors that were interned, prisoners, and they used to give lectures. And then I studied Spanish and Hebrew. So I'm-- I always kept busy, one way or another.

speaker: interviewer
text: It appears to us that all the time when you were traveling, most of the time on the trains you were in passenger trains, that conditions in the camps were certainly better than they were in a lot of the concentration camps. What do you attribute it to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To being British, because, in England, they arrested all the German civilians and they send them to the Isle of Man. It was reciprocal. In every war, they do it. Like here, they arrested the Japanese, and they sent them to camps, which was not right, because the Japanese were American citizens. But, there, I was a British subject. I was the enemy. The same-- the Germans were the enemies in England. So they arrested all the Germans in England and sent them to the Isle of Man as prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by and large, you were arrested as a--

speaker: interviewee
text: As a British subject, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --British national rather than as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jew, yeah. But they knew I was Jewish, because on my identification card, on top, with red ink-- with red pencil, it says 'yud,' so they knew I was Jewish. And we were afraid. We were afraid that maybe, one day, they'll do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other people that you knew in this camp, were many of them Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were about 60, just around 60, Jews. 24 Israelis, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Palestinian.

speaker: interviewee
text: 24 Palestinian Jews, And the rest were-- they were from Belgium, from France, from Holland, all British, British subjects, and I think, from Denmark, and Poland, and some British, real Britishers who lived there all their life, but they were just visiting in Europe when the war broke out, so they were caught as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about mother and dad. They were not British.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were born in Russia and they had Nansen passports, which was-- I don't know about-- it was still-- they were still Russian, but it was international. The passbook was accepted. So they were considered--

speaker: interviewer
text: Non-nationals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-nationals, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so then-- what happened then at the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp, we-- there were the last three months of the war. The-- Right across from-- it's the same camp, but had barbed wire in between, was a big warehouse from the Germans, where they kept the paperwork, and typewriters, and all kinds of utensils, pots, and pans. Supply-- it was supply department of the army. And the last two weeks of the war-- no, even before that-- the British and American planes used to come over to bomb. And they bombed all around the camp, and they bombed this supply house. And we-- believe me, we had no ditches to hide in, nothing. And I remember I got diarrhea again from fright, just from fright because the bombs fell so close, without doing any damage to the British camp. It was unbelievable. They knew the British camp was there. And they bombed the supply house. They bombed it completely, nothing left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so--

speaker: interviewee
text: And everyday, more and more, we used to see more and more British planes come over-- Royal Air Force-- and bomb. And we had-- we were surrounded by-- by mountains like here, like Los Angeles. And suddenly, you saw three planes coming over the top of the mountain, and the speed was terrific, for those days, even. And, in a way, we used to enjoy it, because we knew they were coming to bomb the Germans. But we were frightened. Everybody was frightened. I remember, the last week of the war, I couldn't eat anything. I was sick just knowing that the end was near.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we heard, on May the 8th, that the war was over, suddenly. So we had a few tough guys go to the gate and tell them to open the gates. They said, no-- the Germans-- no, we can't open the gates because we have instructions to keep it closed till somebody-- oh, yes, and the man from the YMCA from Sweden was there. So he had something-- and he told us the same thing. He said, listen, here, it's better for you to stay in the camp till somebody liberates you. It's not good to go, just to go out. But we have-- we had a few tough guys as prisoners, from the Marines, the British Marines. And the first day, they listened. The second day, they said no. The war's over, and I'm-- I want to leave the camp. So they went to the guard, and they said you are opening it, otherwise we'll open it. And he got frightened, and he opened the gates. So they went out. Everybody went out, I, as well. I went any place. As I said, we came to a Russian camp not far away from there. We couldn't speak a word with them, but at least everybody was happy. And when I came back, on the way back, suddenly, I saw an enormous load of planes coming-- British planes. And they were dropping big cases from the air by parachute. And they were supplies-- bread and other foods. And I, amongst all the others, went and took them off the hooks, you know, and dragged them into the camp to eat. We had food, and we had medicines. They sent medicines over. And we were told that the army's coming in in the afternoon. And so it was, the afternoon. The first--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this YMCA chap. How did he show up one day?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came, and he had credentials. I don't know, from the Germans, actually. And he had instructions to take-- to stay in the camp, in this particular camp, and hand it over to whoever will take over, will liberate the camp. So he lived with us for the one week. And then when the war was over, nobody came.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said to the head of the prisoner, we had a head amongst ourselves-- A prisoner?

speaker: interviewee
text: A prisoner, as well. And he negotiated with the Germans whenever there was anything to be negotiated with. And they both said they're going to look for the British or the American army. And they went out and they spent, I believe, a day. And the next day, they found them. They told them, listen, there's a British camp. We were waiting for you to come. And they came. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was-- the feeling, you can't describe the feeling. It's-- to be liberated after-- I was there only just over three years, but some of the other prisoners were there five years. It's a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell us a little about the liberation itself. They come in, and what happens to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came in. First, they dropped the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Supplies?

speaker: interviewee
text: The supplies. Then they came in. They arrested all the Germans. They had to throw their guns and rifles away on the big heap. In fact, I think-- I think there's some pictures there. And they were put on the field with British guards around them. And, naturally, we were happy. I can't tell you the feeling we had. And they told us don't worry. Within the next day or two, we are taking you away from here-- the British. And we had plenty to eat at that time already. And so it was. On the Friday morning, I don't remember what was the-- I think it was the 10th of May, they came with a whole row of trucks. And they loaded us onto them and we left. And we left for a place-- I don't remember the name. It's between the [GERMAN] Spittal and Venice, Italy, in a town in the mountains there. And we stopped there for an hour or two. We got to eat. And there was a big field as well with barbed wire all around and filled with German prisoners. And this was a nice-- a lovely feeling to see it. And in the afternoon, we arrived in Venice, late in the afternoon, not in Venice, in a place called-- just outside Venice-- minutes away from there. We didn't go into Venice proper. And we stopped there for awhile. And then they took us to a camp, a special camp for repatrioting British prisoners in Ancona. That was the next stop we had. And it was lovely. They had banners outside, 'Welcome, Prisoners.' And they gave us lovely food there. The food was cooked food, lovely. And toothbrushes, and shoe brushes, and towels, and all these kinds of things. We stayed there one or two days. I don't remember. On the beach, it was practically on the beach. And then, from there, they loaded us up again on trucks and took us to Rome-- all the way to Rome. We stayed in Rome for nine days, took us on tours to the Vatican. All on trucks, there was no public transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who paid for this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The government, the military, the government, the British government-- I mean, it was the army, British Army. And after nine days, they took us by train to Naples. And we stayed in a resort place near Naples, at the foot of the Vesuvius. We have the Vesuvius right behind us and a lovely home on the beach, beautiful, luxury home. And there, we got some clothes, new clothes. They gave us shots, different shots. Three days, we stayed there. And there, they put us on the boat, took us back to Naples, put us on a boat. And they shipped us to Scotland. On the military-- it was all on the navy, marine. They took us all the way to Scotland, where we landed in a place called Greenock, in Scotland. And we arrived there on Saturday morning. On the weekend, they don't work. On the weekend, they don't work, so we had to stay on board the ship till Monday morning. Monday morning, they send us to London. They gave us a ticket, a railway ticket, from Glasgow to London.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived in London, and then what happens?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the boat in Greenock, in Scotland, everyone had to come before an officer to give your name and your story, whatever. And this officer, when I came to him, he said Hyman Skulsky, you're going to your cousin's, Rabbi Swartz, in London. You're staying with them. I said, how do you know? I was so surprised. We inquired before you came, he said. A long time ago, you gave them as your next of kin in England. He said, we inquired. They say they want you to come soon as you're back. So I knew where to go. I went to my cousin's and I had a big reception there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then your life starts in London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then my life started in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had other cousins there, whom I was very close with. And the first day I came, I called them up, actually, to tell them I'm back. And they were happy, I can't tell you how. So she said, come over, you've got to come over right away. I said, not today. Today, I'm too tired. Tomorrow, I'll come over. Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came the next day. And after being three years in the camp, and my cousin was there. She was a woman about, at that time, probably around 60-ish. And there were two or three other women there-- her friends and her daughter. And first, naturally, I had to tell my story. This was the beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, then, the discussion went over to hats. And they started discussing fashion, about hats they're wearing and what-- you know. And I was sitting there and listening. I couldn't believe it. You know, it was so, like, in a different world. Here, you come from a camp in Germany after so many things had happened, and you come back there, and suddenly they're discussing--

speaker: interviewer
text: Fashion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fashion. I couldn't-- it didn't-- it couldn't enter my mind. It was just-- it was too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what happens in London with your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spent-- I wanted to go back to Belgium right away. My two brothers had-- were in Belgium already, from Switzerland. They had been in Switzerland. So there was no public transport. It didn't exist. You couldn't go from England to Belgium. So after a while, I find-- I found out I could go back to Belgium with the military, with the navy. So I applied. They said, yes. They didn't charge me anything. And I went on one of their navy or marine boats, and they took me back to Belgium. I came back to-- I stayed with my-- one of my brothers, naturally. They'd been in Switzerland for three years, also prisoners, but not the same. And, gradually, I didn't know what to do. I had no idea what I was going to do at first. I had-- by the way, in London, when I was in London, when I arrived in London, I had a cousin who-- who was a surgeon in Norway. And he had escaped. All the Jews from Norway escaped one night to Sweden. Sweden was not in the war. And he was amongst them. And he joined the military. He became a lieutenant colonel in the British Army, but under-- with Norwegian flag. He was a big shot in London, in the army. And he was there when I came. So he came to see me right away. And he gave me some money, which I had nothing. And he helped me out quite a bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're back in Antwerp now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happens?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I couldn't find my place in Antwerp either. I just didn't know. It wasn't the same. When you come back here, my cousin lived here. My aunt used to live, and all my friends were killed. You know, it's coming back like-- in a way, like coming to a cemetery. At that time, it was terrible, first visit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I tried-- my brother was working. He was cutting diamonds. And he had a brother-in-law who was also cutting diamonds in his place. They worked together. So one day, he said, you know what, you want to learn to do it? I said, good. So they taught me how to cut diamonds as well for a while, but I didn't like it. I just didn't-- it wasn't my-- so I did all kind of businesses, white business and black business, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- first of all, I was mad at the world. When I came back, I couldn't believe that the world could do such a thing, not just the Germans. The Germans were the-- they actually did it, but many other parts of the world were just as guilty, because they could've lifted a finger. They didn't, including England. They did the same. They didn't care. Nobody cared about the Jews. So, anyway, I couldn't find my place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you dabbled in a lot of different businesses.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, I went back in my business. Then my brother also started another business. He made bags. There was a-- plastic came out. It was a new thing, plastic, you know, beginning. So everybody went into this business. So he also started to manufacture special bags. And I used to go out and sell them, or try to sell. And then, one day, I decided I'm going to Israel. But this was in '49 already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you decide to move to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Israel. So when I came to Israel, I had a rough time there as well. I mean rough. I didn't expect anything good. There was only one good thing that I did. On the spur of the moment, I knew there was no housing there. You couldn't get a place to live there. It's very expensive. And thousands of people coming, so it got worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I designed a pre-fabricated bungalow for myself. I bought the material in Belgium. And I also had furniture. I had some furniture there, which I had bought. So I put everything in a lift, in a case, and I shipped it to Israel. And I said I'll find a place where to put it up. And I couldn't find a job. I'm an electrician by trade. So one day, I get-- I go to this [NON-ENGLISH] and I ask them I'd like to work as an electrician. You've got to go for a test. I said, good. So they send me. One morning, at 7 o'clock, I have to come to a man who's an electrician. He's got to give me the test. I come up to him, in the private home, in his kitchen. He's having his breakfast. And he says, what wire do we use here in Israel? I said I don't know in Israel. I know in Belgium what wires, so I told him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, no, that's no good. And another socket, the thing here, you can't work here. You don't know what wire. I said I want-- I want to learn. I don't care. I know how do the job, so I'll use the right wire. I'll find-- no, it's no good. I didn't pass the exam.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of job did you finally get?

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I was in London, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with my cousins, this Rabbi Swartz, one of their cousins from Israel, an Israeli, had come to London with a baby. And she had this-- her husband was a non-Jewish soldier, an Englishman, and he got killed in the end of the war. So she came to London to join her other relatives with the baby. And you know, I had nothing to do there. I was a newcomer. So I helped her, going shopping. She-- she was sick at the time, as well. So I went to buy things for her, she needed. And I helped her as much as I could. When I came to Israel, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was looking for a job. Every afternoon, I used to go to a place called the Noga. It's a little coffee shop where everybody used to go to just sit there with a coffee or a piece of cake.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day, I was sitting there. And this was about two months, three months, after I'd arrived. A woman comes up to me. She says, Hym. I said, yes, and I look at her. And then, suddenly, I remember this was the cousin that I helped in London. She was back living again in Israel. What are you doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't know I was-- I was there, and I didn't know that she was there. She said, come, join us. So I come down, sit down at her table, at this coffee shop. And she introduces me-- oh, she was sitting with one man, yes, Mr. Fell.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, oh, hello, Mr. Fell, how do you do? He said are you doing anything. He said to me. I said, no, I'm-- I've just come a couple of months ago. I can't find a job. So far, nothing. So I turned to him, and I said, what about you? Are you working? He says, yes, I'm working. I say, what do you do? He says, I'm the British consul here, just like this. British consul, oh, very nice, very interesting. She worked at the consulate. She got the job as the receptionist at the consulate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she had become very friendly with him. So when he said this, she says to me, Hym, I'll tell you what. Come to the consulate tomorrow. They need a driver. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I said driver . I didn't even consider it, you know? But I was looking for work, I mean, after all. So come tomorrow at 1 o'clock. OK. So I went at 1 o'clock. So she tells me last-- two weeks ago, one of their drivers went swimming, and he drowned. Which was true, this was a fact. She say then now they need another driver. Put your application in. OK.
